text.title: CD Core Principles
text.summary: Four concrete rules that make Continuous Delivery work: releasable commits, automated pipeline stages, full automation scope, and feature-flag decoupling of deploy from release.

rule.statement: Every commit that passes automated tests must be deployable to production without manual testing or code changes.
rule.rationale: Manual testing gates batch risk into releases; eliminating them forces small, backward-compatible changes and reduces deployment stress.

rule.statement: Use feature flags to ship code dark — deploy code to production without making the feature visible to users.
rule.rationale: Decoupling deployment from release eliminates the biggest CD obstacle: incomplete features blocking ship dates. Deploy is an engineering event; release is a product event.

rule.statement: Keep all database migrations backward-compatible; split schema changes from application changes across separate deployments.
rule.rationale: Non-backward-compatible migrations require coordinated application+schema deploys, creating a manual gate that breaks CD.

rule.statement: The pipeline must flow: Commit → Build → Test (unit + integration + E2E) → Stage → Production. No stage may be skipped; each stage gates the next.
rule.rationale: Skipping stages creates a false sense of coverage and produces the same batching problem CD is designed to prevent.

rule.statement: Keep total pipeline time under 15 minutes p95; longer pipelines incentivize batching commits to reduce pipeline frequency.
rule.rationale: A 45-minute pipeline creates pressure to wait until "the pipeline slot is worth it," which reintroduces large batches.

rule.statement: Store pipeline YAML in the same repository as the application code; no out-of-band CI jobs managed separately from the codebase.
rule.rationale: Out-of-band pipelines cannot be reviewed in PRs, cannot be tested in branches, and diverge from the application they serve.

rule.statement: Automate: build, test execution, environment provisioning, database migrations, configuration management, deployment, monitoring setup, and rollback. Manual steps are only production deploy trigger (CD) or nothing (Continuous Deployment).
rule.rationale: Any unautomated step becomes a bottleneck, a source of human error, and a reason teams defer releases.

rule.statement: Every deployment must have a trivial, tested rollback path; require rollback rehearsal before considering a service CD-ready.
rule.rationale: CD without rollback is high-risk; the ability to undo any deployment in minutes is what makes frequent deploys safe.
